title: University of California

infobox.value: [Let there be light](/wiki/Let_there_be_light "Let there be light")

[Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California") (Office of the President) , California , United States  [37°48′08″N 122°16′17″W﻿ / ﻿37.8022°N 122.2714°W﻿ / 37.8022; -122.2714](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=University_of_California&params=37.8022_N_122.2714_W_region:US-CA_type:edu)
10 campuses under direct control (9 with undergraduate and graduate schools, one professional/graduate only), one affiliated law school, and one national laboratory

(**UC**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [land-grant](/wiki/Land-grant_university "Land-grant university") [research](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") [university system](/wiki/University_system "University system") in the U.S. state of [California](/wiki/California "California"). Headquartered in [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California"), the system is composed of its ten campuses at [Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"), [Davis](/wiki/University_of_California,_Davis "University of California, Davis"), [Irvine](/wiki/University_of_California,_Irvine "University of California, Irvine"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles"), [Merced](/wiki/University_of_California,_Merced "University of California, Merced"), [Riverside](/wiki/University_of_California,_Riverside "University of California, Riverside"), [San Diego](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego"), [San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Francisco "University of California, San Francisco"), [Santa Barbara](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara"), and [Santa Cruz](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Cruz "University of California, Santa Cruz"), along with numerous research centers and academic centers abroad.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The system is the state's land-grant university.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The **University of California** (**UC**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [land-grant](/wiki/Land-grant_university "Land-grant university") [research](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") [university system](/wiki/University_system "University system") in the U.S. state of [California](/wiki/California "California"). Headquartered in [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California"), the system is composed of its ten campuses at [Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"), [Davis](/wiki/University_of_California,_Davis "University of California, Davis"), [Irvine](/wiki/University_of_California,_Irvine "University of California, Irvine"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles"), [Merced](/wiki/University_of_California,_Merced "University of California, Merced"), [Riverside](/wiki/University_of_California,_Riverside "University of California, Riverside"), [San Diego](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego"), [San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Francisco "University of California, San Francisco"), [Santa Barbara](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara"), and [Santa Cruz](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Cruz "University of California, Santa Cruz"), along with numerous research centers and academic centers abroad.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The system is the state's land-grant university.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 1900, UC was one of the founders of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") and since the 1970s seven of its campuses, in addition to Berkeley, have been admitted to the association. Berkeley, Davis, Irvine, Los Angeles, Santa Barbara, Santa Cruz, Riverside, and San Diego are considered [Public Ivies](/wiki/Public_Ivy "Public Ivy"), making California the state with the most universities in the nation to hold the title.[[6]](#cite_note-:11-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) UC campuses have large numbers of distinguished [faculty](/wiki/Faculty_(academic_staff) "Faculty (academic staff)") in almost every [academic discipline](/wiki/Discipline_(academia) "Discipline (academia)"), with UC faculty and researchers having won 75 [Nobel Prizes](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") as of 2025.[[8]](#cite_note-UC_Regents-8)

The system's ten campuses have a combined student body of 299,407 students, 26,100 faculty members, 192,400 staff members and over 2.5 million [alumni](/wiki/Alumnus "Alumnus").[[2]](#cite_note-At_a_glance-2) Its newest campus in Merced opened in fall 2005. Nine campuses enroll both [undergraduate](/wiki/Undergraduate_education "Undergraduate education") and [graduate](/wiki/Graduate_school "Graduate school") students; one campus, UC San Francisco, enrolls only graduate and professional students in the medical and health sciences. In addition, the [University of California College of the Law](/wiki/University_of_California_College_of_the_Law,_San_Francisco "University of California College of the Law, San Francisco") located in San Francisco is legally affiliated with UC and shares its name but is otherwise autonomous. Under the [California Master Plan for Higher Education](/wiki/California_Master_Plan_for_Higher_Education "California Master Plan for Higher Education"), the University of California is a part of the state's three-system public higher education plan, which also includes the [California State University](/wiki/California_State_University "California State University") system and the [California Community Colleges](/wiki/California_Community_Colleges "California Community Colleges") system. UC is governed by a [Board of Regents](/wiki/University_of_California_Regents "University of California Regents") whose autonomy from the rest of the state government is protected by the state constitution.[[9]](#cite_note-Grodin_Page243-9) The University of California also manages or co-manages three national laboratories for the [U.S. Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy"): [Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory") (LBNL), [Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Livermore_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory") (LLNL), and [Los Alamos National Laboratory](/wiki/Los_Alamos_National_Laboratory "Los Alamos National Laboratory") (LANL).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The University of California was founded on March 23, 1868, and operated in Oakland, where it absorbed the assets of the [College of California](/wiki/College_of_California "College of California") before moving to [Berkeley](/wiki/Berkeley,_California "Berkeley, California") in 1873.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) It also affiliated itself with independent medical and law schools in San Francisco. Over the next eight decades, several branch locations and satellite programs were established across the state. In March 1951, the University of California began to reorganize itself into something distinct from its campus in Berkeley, with UC president [Robert Gordon Sproul](/wiki/Robert_Gordon_Sproul "Robert Gordon Sproul") staying in place as chief executive of the UC system, while [Clark Kerr](/wiki/Clark_Kerr "Clark Kerr") became Berkeley's first chancellor[[13]](#cite_note-Stadtman2-13)[[14]](#cite_note-MargaretLeslieDavis-14)[[15]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr1-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) and [Raymond B. Allen](/wiki/Raymond_B._Allen "Raymond B. Allen") became the first chancellor of UCLA.[[17]](#cite_note-17) However, the 1951 reorganization was stalled by resistance from Sproul and his allies,[[18]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr2-18) and it was not until Kerr succeeded Sproul as UC president that UC was able to evolve into a university system from 1957 to 1960.[[19]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr3-19) At that time, chancellors were appointed for additional campuses and each was granted some degree of greater autonomy.[[20]](#cite_note-Trombley-20)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Bay_Blue_-_Flickr_-_Joe_Parks.jpg/250px-Bay_Blue_-_Flickr_-_Joe_Parks.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bay_Blue_-_Flickr_-_Joe_Parks.jpg)

In November 1857, the [College of California](/wiki/College_of_California "College of California")'s trustees began to acquire various parcels of land facing the [Golden Gate](/wiki/Golden_Gate "Golden Gate") in what is now [Berkeley](/wiki/Berkeley,_California "Berkeley, California").

In 1849, the state of California ratified its first constitution, which contained the express objective of creating a complete educational system including a state university.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Taking advantage of the [Morrill Land-Grant Acts](/wiki/Morrill_Land-Grant_Acts "Morrill Land-Grant Acts"), the [California State Legislature](/wiki/California_State_Legislature "California State Legislature") established an Agricultural, Mining, and Mechanical Arts College in 1866.[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Marsden-23) However, it existed only on paper, as a placeholder to secure federal [land-grant funds](/wiki/Land-grant_university "Land-grant university").[[23]](#cite_note-Marsden-23)

Meanwhile, [Congregational](/wiki/Congregational "Congregational") [minister](/wiki/Minister_of_religion "Minister of religion") [Henry Durant](/wiki/Henry_Durant "Henry Durant"), an alumnus of [Yale](/wiki/Yale "Yale"), had established the private Contra Costa Academy, on June 20, 1853, in [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California"), California.[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22) The initial site was bounded by Twelfth and Fourteenth Streets and Harrison and Franklin Streets in [downtown Oakland](/wiki/Downtown_Oakland "Downtown Oakland")[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22) (and is marked today by State Historical Plaque No. 45 at the northeast corner of Thirteenth and Franklin). In turn, the academy's trustees were granted a charter in 1855 for a [College of California](/wiki/College_of_California "College of California"), though the college continued to operate as a [college preparatory school](/wiki/University-preparatory_school "University-preparatory school") until it added college-level courses in 1860.[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Marsden-23) The college's trustees, educators, and supporters believed in the importance of a [liberal arts education](/wiki/Liberal_arts_education "Liberal arts education") (especially the study of the Greek and Roman [classics](/wiki/Classics "Classics")), but ran into a lack of interest in [liberal arts colleges](/wiki/Liberal_arts_college "Liberal arts college") on the [American frontier](/wiki/American_frontier "American frontier") (for [post-secondary](/wiki/Tertiary_education "Tertiary education") degrees, the college was graduating only three or four students per year).[[23]](#cite_note-Marsden-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/South_Hall_UC_Berkeley.jpg/250px-South_Hall_UC_Berkeley.jpg)](/wiki/File:South_Hall_UC_Berkeley.jpg)

[South Hall](/wiki/South_Hall_(UC_Berkeley) "South Hall (UC Berkeley)"), built in 1873, is the oldest building on the [Berkeley](/wiki/UC_Berkeley "UC Berkeley") campus.

In November 1857, the college's trustees began to acquire various parcels of land facing the [Golden Gate](/wiki/Golden_Gate "Golden Gate") in what is now [Berkeley](/wiki/Berkeley,_California "Berkeley, California") for a future planned campus to the north of Oakland.[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22) But first, they needed to secure the college's water rights by buying a large farm to the east.[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22) In 1864, they organized the College Homestead Association, which borrowed $35,000 to purchase the land, plus another $33,000 to purchase 160 acres (650,000 m2) of [land to the south of the future campus](/wiki/Southside,_Berkeley,_California "Southside, Berkeley, California").[[24]](#cite_note-24) The association subdivided the latter parcel and started selling lots with the hope it could raise enough money to repay its lenders and also create a new [college town](/wiki/College_town "College town").[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22) But sales of new homesteads fell short.[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22)

Governor [Frederick Low](/wiki/Frederick_Low "Frederick Low") favored the establishment of a state university based upon the [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan") plan, and thus in one sense may be regarded as the founder of the University of California.[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Marsden-23) At the College of California's 1867 [commencement exercises](/wiki/Graduation "Graduation"), where Low was present, [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") professor [Benjamin Silliman Jr.](/wiki/Benjamin_Silliman_Jr. "Benjamin Silliman Jr.") criticized Californians for establishing a [polytechnic school](/wiki/Institute_of_technology "Institute of technology"), instead of a real university.[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Marsden-23) That same day, Low reportedly first suggested a merger of the already-functional College of California (which had a liberal arts focus, land, buildings, faculty, and students, but not enough money) with the nonfunctional state college (which had a polytechnical focus, money and nothing else), and went on to participate in the ensuing negotiations.[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Marsden-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/UCSF_1908_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-UCSF_1908_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:UCSF_1908_(cropped).jpg)

[UC San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Francisco "University of California, San Francisco") campus in 1908.

On October 9, 1867, the college's trustees reluctantly agreed to join forces with the state college to their mutual advantage, but under one condition—that there not be simply an "Agricultural, Mining, and Mechanical Arts College", but a complete university, within which the assets of the College of California would be used to create a College of Letters (now known as the [College of Letters and Science](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_College_of_Letters_and_Science "UC Berkeley College of Letters and Science")).[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Marsden-23)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Accordingly, the Organic Act, establishing the University of California, was introduced as a bill by [Assemblyman](/wiki/California_State_Assembly "California State Assembly") [John W. Dwinelle](/wiki/John_W._Dwinelle "John W. Dwinelle") on March 5, 1868, and after it was duly passed by both houses of the state legislature, it was signed into [state law](/wiki/Law_of_California "Law of California") by Governor [Henry H. Haight](/wiki/Henry_H._Haight "Henry H. Haight") (Low's successor) on March 23, 1868.[[22]](#cite_note-Stadtman-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Marsden-23)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

However, as legally constituted, the new university was *not* an actual merger of the two colleges, but was an entirely new institution which merely inherited certain objectives and assets from each of them.[[27]](#cite_note-Stadtman_Page_34-27) Governor Haight saw no need to honor any tacit understandings reached with his predecessor about institutional continuity.[[23]](#cite_note-Marsden-23) Only two college trustees became regents and a single faculty member ([Martin Kellogg](/wiki/Martin_Kellogg "Martin Kellogg")) was hired by the new university.[[23]](#cite_note-Marsden-23) By April 1869, the trustees had second thoughts about their agreement to donate the college's assets and disincorporate. To get them to proceed, regent [John B. Felton](/wiki/John_B._Felton "John B. Felton") helped them bring a "friendly suit" against the university to test the agreement's legality—which they promptly lost.[[28]](#cite_note-Stadtman_Page_39-28)

The University of California's second president, [Daniel Coit Gilman](/wiki/Daniel_Coit_Gilman "Daniel Coit Gilman"), opened [its new campus](/wiki/Campus_of_the_University_of_California,_Berkeley "Campus of the University of California, Berkeley") in Berkeley in September 1873.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### UC affiliates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: UC affiliates")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/A_Gary_Anderson_Hall%2C_UCR_%28cropped2%29.JPG/250px-A_Gary_Anderson_Hall%2C_UCR_%28cropped2%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:A_Gary_Anderson_Hall,_UCR_(cropped2).JPG)

The [Citrus Experiment Station](/wiki/University_of_California_Citrus_Experiment_Station "University of California Citrus Experiment Station"), built in 1917, is the oldest building on the [UC Riverside](/wiki/UC_Riverside "UC Riverside") campus.

Section 8 of the Organic Act authorized the Board of Regents to affiliate the University of California with independent self-sustaining professional colleges.[[30]](#cite_note-Stadtman4-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) "Affiliation" meant UC and its affiliates would "share the risk in launching new endeavors in education".[[30]](#cite_note-Stadtman4-30) The affiliates shared the prestige of the state university's brand, and UC agreed to award degrees in its own name to their graduates on the recommendation of their respective faculties, but the affiliates were otherwise managed independently by their own boards of trustees, charged their own tuition and fees, and maintained their own budgets separate from the UC budget.[[30]](#cite_note-Stadtman4-30) It was through the process of affiliation that UC was able to claim it had medical and law schools in San Francisco within a decade of its founding.[[30]](#cite_note-Stadtman4-30)

In 1879, California adopted its second and [current constitution](/wiki/California_Constitution "California Constitution"), which included unusually strong language to ensure UC's independence from the rest of the [state government](/wiki/Government_of_California "Government of California").[[9]](#cite_note-Grodin_Page243-9)[[32]](#cite_note-CalConsArt9Sec9-32) This had lasting consequences for the [Hastings College of the Law](/wiki/University_of_California_College_of_the_Law,_San_Francisco "University of California College of the Law, San Francisco"), which had been separately chartered and affiliated in 1878 by an act of the state legislature at the behest of founder [Serranus Clinton Hastings](/wiki/Serranus_Clinton_Hastings "Serranus Clinton Hastings").[[33]](#cite_note-BarnesPages447172-33) After a falling out with his own handpicked board of directors, the founder persuaded the state legislature in 1883 and 1885 to pass new laws to place his law school under the direct control of the Board of Regents.[[34]](#cite_note-BarnesPage7882-34) In 1886, the [Supreme Court of California](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_California "Supreme Court of California") declared those newer acts to be unconstitutional because the clause protecting UC's independence in the 1879 state constitution had stripped the state legislature of the ability to amend the 1878 act.[[35]](#cite_note-BarnesPage8485-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) To this day, the College of the Law (which dropped Hastings from its name in 2023) remains a UC affiliate, maintains its own board of directors, and is not governed by the regents.[[30]](#cite_note-Stadtman4-30)[[35]](#cite_note-BarnesPage8485-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Animal_Science_Building%2C_UC_Davis_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Animal_Science_Building%2C_UC_Davis_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Animal_Science_Building,_UC_Davis_(cropped).jpg)

[Hart Hall](/wiki/George_Hart_Hall "George Hart Hall") at [UC Davis](/wiki/UC_Davis "UC Davis"), built in 1928, is listed in the National Register of Historic Places.

In contrast, [Toland Medical College](/wiki/Toland_Medical_College "Toland Medical College") (founded in 1864 and affiliated in 1873) and later, the dental, pharmacy, and nursing schools in San Francisco were affiliated with UC through written agreements, and not statutes invested with constitutional importance by court decisions.[[30]](#cite_note-Stadtman4-30) In the early 20th century, the Affiliated Colleges (as they came to be called) began to agree to submit to the regents' governance during the term of President [Benjamin Ide Wheeler](/wiki/Benjamin_Ide_Wheeler "Benjamin Ide Wheeler"), as the Board of Regents had come to recognize the problems inherent in the existence of independent entities that shared the UC brand but over which UC had no real control.[[30]](#cite_note-Stadtman4-30) While Hastings remained independent, the Affiliated Colleges were able to increasingly coordinate their operations with one another under the supervision of the UC president and regents, and evolved into the health sciences campus known today as the University of California, San Francisco.[[30]](#cite_note-Stadtman4-30)

### Becoming a research university

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Becoming a research university")]

Section 1 of the Organic Act authorized the university to "provide instruction and complete education" in many different fields and professions,[[37]](#cite_note-37) but the text of the Organic Act is notably silent about [research](/wiki/Research "Research").[[38]](#cite_note-Stadtman_Page_509-38) It was not until the 1930s, during the administration of President Sproul, that UC's mission drifted away from its traditional focus on instruction—which became the province of the California State University—and towards research.[[38]](#cite_note-Stadtman_Page_509-38) Sproul started to speak of UC's missions as "teaching, research, and public service",[[38]](#cite_note-Stadtman_Page_509-38) which remains true today.[[39]](#cite_note-UC_Mission-39) Thus, UC evolved into a research university whose faculty and staff would perform research to contribute directly to society, as opposed to indirect contributions by instructing students to equip them with the skills needed to later perform research in their own careers.[[38]](#cite_note-Stadtman_Page_509-38) The Master Plan for Higher Education, as enacted into state law in 1960, provides that UC "shall be the primary state-supported academic agency for research".[[40]](#cite_note-section66010.4-40)

### North-south tensions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: North-south tensions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Powell_Library_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Powell_Library_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Powell_Library_(cropped).JPG)

[Powell Library](/wiki/Powell_Library "Powell Library"), built in 1929, is one of the four oldest buildings on the [UCLA](/wiki/UCLA "UCLA") campus.

In August 1882, the [California State Normal School](/wiki/California_State_Normal_School "California State Normal School") (whose original [normal school](/wiki/Normal_school "Normal school") in [San Jose](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California") is now [San Jose State University](/wiki/San_Jose_State_University "San Jose State University")) opened a second school in Los Angeles to train teachers for the growing population of Southern California.[[41]](#cite_note-41) In 1887, the Los Angeles school was granted its own board of trustees independent of the San Jose school.[[42]](#cite_note-Gerth_Page_26-42) The state constitution prohibited the legislature from directly commanding the Board of Regents to create a southern campus, but it did not prohibit the state legislature from passing legislation to create additional state universities, and the supporters of the Los Angeles State Normal School used the possibility of that scenario to pressure the Board of Regents to voluntarily accept the normal school as UC's southern campus.[[43]](#cite_note-Dundjerski_Page_15-43) In 1919, the state legislature transferred the normal school to UC control and renamed it the Southern Branch of the University of California.[[44]](#cite_note-Dundjerski_Page_21-44) In 1927, it became the [University of California at Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles"); the "at" would be replaced with a comma in 1958.[[45]](#cite_note-Dundjerski_Page_46-45)

Los Angeles surpassed San Francisco in the [1920 census](/wiki/1920_United_States_census "1920 United States census") to become the most populous metropolis in California. Because Los Angeles had become the state government's single largest source of both tax revenue and votes, its residents felt entitled to demand more prestige and autonomy for their campus. Their efforts bore fruit in March 1951, when UCLA became the first UC site outside of Berkeley to achieve *de jure* coequal status with the Berkeley campus. That month, the regents approved a reorganization plan under which both the Berkeley and Los Angeles campuses would be supervised by chancellors reporting to the UC president.[[13]](#cite_note-Stadtman2-13)[[14]](#cite_note-MargaretLeslieDavis-14)[[15]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr1-15)[[46]](#cite_note-ChancellorBylaw-46) However, the 1951 plan was severely flawed; it was overly vague about how the chancellors were to become the "executive heads" of their campuses. Due to stubborn resistance from President Sproul and several vice presidents and deans—who simply carried on as before—the chancellors ended up as glorified [provosts](/wiki/Provost_(education) "Provost (education)") with limited control over academic affairs and long-range planning while the president and the regents retained *de facto* control over everything else.[[18]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr2-18)

### Transformation and decentralization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transformation and decentralization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Campus_of_the_University_of_California%2C_Irvine_%28aerial_view%2C_circa_2006%29.jpg/250px-Campus_of_the_University_of_California%2C_Irvine_%28aerial_view%2C_circa_2006%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Campus_of_the_University_of_California,_Irvine_(aerial_view,_circa_2006).jpg)

[UC Irvine](/wiki/UC_Irvine "UC Irvine") was founded and had its campus built out in the 1960s.

Upon becoming president in October 1957, Clark Kerr supervised UC's rapid transformation into a true public university system through a series of proposals adopted unanimously by the regents from 1957 to 1960.[[19]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr3-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Trombley-20) Kerr's reforms included expressly granting all campus chancellors the full range of executive powers, privileges, and responsibilities which Sproul had denied to Kerr himself, as well as the radical decentralization of a tightly knit bureaucracy in which all lines of authority had always run directly to the president at Berkeley or to the regents themselves.[[19]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr3-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Trombley-20)[[46]](#cite_note-ChancellorBylaw-46) In 1965, UCLA Chancellor [Franklin D. Murphy](/wiki/Franklin_David_Murphy "Franklin David Murphy") tried to push this to what he saw as its logical conclusion: he advocated for authorizing all chancellors to report directly to the Board of Regents, thereby rendering the UC president redundant.[[47]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr6-47) Murphy wanted to transform UC from one federated university into a confederation of independent universities, similar to [the situation in Kansas](/wiki/Kansas_Board_of_Regents "Kansas Board of Regents") (from where he was recruited).[[47]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr6-47) Murphy was unable to develop any support for his proposal, Kerr quickly put down what he thought of as "Murphy's rebellion", and therefore Kerr's vision of UC as a university system prevailed: "one university with pluralistic decision-making".[[47]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr6-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Geisel_Library%2C_UCSD.jpg/250px-Geisel_Library%2C_UCSD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Geisel_Library,_UCSD.jpg)

[Geisel Library](/wiki/Geisel_Library "Geisel Library"), at [UC San Diego](/wiki/UC_San_Diego "UC San Diego"), was built in 1970.

During the 20th century, UC acquired additional satellite locations which, like Los Angeles, were all subordinate to administrators at the Berkeley campus. California farmers lobbied for UC to perform [applied research](/wiki/Applied_research "Applied research") responsive to their immediate needs; in 1905, the Legislature established a "University Farm School" at [Davis](/wiki/Davis,_California "Davis, California") and in 1907 a "Citrus Experiment Station" at [Riverside](/wiki/Riverside,_California "Riverside, California") as adjuncts to the College of Agriculture at Berkeley. In 1912, UC acquired a [private oceanography laboratory](/wiki/Scripps_Institution_of_Oceanography "Scripps Institution of Oceanography") in San Diego, which had been founded nine years earlier by local business promoters working with a Berkeley professor. In 1944, UC acquired Santa Barbara State College from the California State Colleges, the descendants of the State Normal Schools.[[48]](#cite_note-Gerth-48) In 1958, the regents began promoting these locations to general campuses, thereby creating [UCSB](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara") (1958), [UC Davis](/wiki/University_of_California,_Davis "University of California, Davis") (1959), [UC Riverside](/wiki/University_of_California,_Riverside "University of California, Riverside") (1959), [UC San Diego](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego") (1960), and [UCSF](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Francisco "University of California, San Francisco") (1964).[[49]](#cite_note-Stadtman3-49)[[50]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr4-50) Each campus was also granted the right to have its own chancellor upon promotion. In response to California's continued population growth, UC opened two additional general campuses in 1965, with [UC Irvine](/wiki/University_of_California,_Irvine "University of California, Irvine") opening in [Irvine](/wiki/Irvine,_California "Irvine, California") and [UC Santa Cruz](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Cruz "University of California, Santa Cruz") opening in [Santa Cruz](/wiki/Santa_Cruz,_California "Santa Cruz, California").[[49]](#cite_note-Stadtman3-49) The youngest campus, [UC Merced](/wiki/University_of_California,_Merced "University of California, Merced") opened in fall 2005 to serve the [San Joaquin Valley](/wiki/San_Joaquin_Valley "San Joaquin Valley").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/UCSC_Science_Hill_2017-05-05.jpg/250px-UCSC_Science_Hill_2017-05-05.jpg)](/wiki/File:UCSC_Science_Hill_2017-05-05.jpg)

[UC Santa Cruz](/wiki/UC_Santa_Cruz "UC Santa Cruz"), founded in 1965.

After losing campuses in Los Angeles and Santa Barbara to the University of California system, supporters of the California State College system arranged for the state constitution to be amended in 1946 to prevent similar losses from happening again in the future.[[48]](#cite_note-Gerth-48)

With decentralization complete, it was decided in 1986 that the UC president should no longer be based at the Berkeley campus, and the UC Office of the President moved to [Kaiser Center](/wiki/Kaiser_Center "Kaiser Center") in Oakland in 1989.[[51]](#cite_note-Johnson_Page_84-51) That lakefront location was subject to widespread criticism as "too elegant and too corporate for a public university".[[52]](#cite_note-Pelfrey_Page_55-52) In 1998, the Office of the President moved again, to a newly constructed but much more modest building near the former site of the College of California in Oakland.[[53]](#cite_note-Pelfrey_Page_180-53)

### Modern history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Modern history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/UCMSciEng_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-UCMSciEng_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:UCMSciEng_(cropped).JPG)

[UC Merced](/wiki/UC_Merced "UC Merced"), founded in 2005.

The Master Plan for Higher Education of 1960 established that UC must admit undergraduates from the top 12.5% (one-eighth) of graduating high school seniors in California. Prior to the promulgation of the Master Plan, UC was to admit undergraduates from the top 15%. UC does not currently adhere to all tenets of the original Master Plan, such as the directives that no campus was to exceed total enrollment of 27,500 students (in order to ensure quality) and that public higher education should be [tuition-free](/wiki/College_tuition_in_the_United_States "College tuition in the United States") for California residents. Five campuses, Berkeley, Davis, Irvine, Los Angeles, and San Diego, each have current total enrollment at over 30,000, and of these five, all but Irvine have undergraduate enrollments over 30,000.[[54]](#cite_note-Enrollment-54)

After the state electorate severely limited long-term [property tax](/wiki/Property_tax "Property tax") revenue by enacting [Proposition 13](/wiki/California_Proposition_13_(1978) "California Proposition 13 (1978)") in 1978, UC was forced to make up for the resulting collapse in state financial support by imposing a variety of fees which were tuition in all but name.[[55]](#cite_note-Lindsay-55)[[56]](#cite_note-Lindsey-56)[[57]](#cite_note-Gordon-57) On November 18, 2010, the regents finally gave up on the longstanding [legal fiction](/wiki/Legal_fiction "Legal fiction") that UC does not charge tuition by renaming the Educational Fee to "Tuition".[[58]](#cite_note-58) As part of its search for funds during the 2000s and 2010s, UC quietly began to admit higher percentages of highly accomplished (and more lucrative) students from other states and countries,[[59]](#cite_note-Warren-59) but was forced to reverse course in 2015 in response to the inevitable public outcry and start admitting more California residents.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

On November 14, 2022, about 48,000 academic workers at all ten UC campuses, as well as the [Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory"), went on [strike for higher pay and benefits](/wiki/2022_University_of_California_academic_workers%27_strike "2022 University of California academic workers' strike") as authorized by the [United Auto Workers](/wiki/United_Auto_Workers "United Auto Workers") (UAW) union.[[62]](#cite_note-62) UAW alleged more than 20 [unfair labor practice](/wiki/Unfair_labor_practice "Unfair labor practice") charges against UC, including unilateral changes in policy and obstructing worker negotiation.[[63]](#cite_note-63) The strike lasted almost six weeks, officially ending on December 23.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Governance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/UC_Office_of_the_President.jpg/250px-UC_Office_of_the_President.jpg)](/wiki/File:UC_Office_of_the_President.jpg)

Office of the President of the University of California, in [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland "Oakland")

All University of California campuses except the College of the Law in San Francisco are governed by the [Regents of the University of California](/wiki/Regents_of_the_University_of_California "Regents of the University of California") as required by the [Constitution of the State of California](/wiki/California_Constitution "California Constitution").[[32]](#cite_note-CalConsArt9Sec9-32) Eighteen regents are appointed by the [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_California "Governor of California") for 12-year terms.[[32]](#cite_note-CalConsArt9Sec9-32) [One member](/wiki/University_of_California_Student_Regent "University of California Student Regent") is a student appointed for a one-year term.[[32]](#cite_note-CalConsArt9Sec9-32) There are also seven *[ex officio](/wiki/List_of_Latin_phrases:_E "List of Latin phrases: E")* members—the governor, [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_California "Lieutenant Governor of California"), speaker of the [State Assembly](/wiki/California_State_Assembly "California State Assembly"), [State Superintendent of Public Instruction](/wiki/California_State_Superintendent_of_Public_Instruction "California State Superintendent of Public Instruction"), president and vice president of the UC alumni associations, and the UC president.[[32]](#cite_note-CalConsArt9Sec9-32) The Academic Senate, made up of faculty members, is empowered by the regents to set academic policies.[[32]](#cite_note-CalConsArt9Sec9-32) In addition, the system-wide faculty chair and vice-chair sit on the Board of Regents as non-voting members.[[32]](#cite_note-CalConsArt9Sec9-32)

### President of the University of California

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: President of the University of California")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Blake_House_%28University_of_California_Presidential_Residence%29_2.jpg/250px-Blake_House_%28University_of_California_Presidential_Residence%29_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blake_House_(University_of_California_Presidential_Residence)_2.jpg)

[Blake House and Gardens](/wiki/Blake_Garden_(Kensington,_California) "Blake Garden (Kensington, California)"), built by architect [Walter Danforth Bliss](/wiki/Walter_Danforth_Bliss "Walter Danforth Bliss") in 1924, served as the official residence of the UC President, from 1967 until 2008, when it was opened to the public.

Originally, the president was the chief executive of the first campus, Berkeley. In turn, other UC locations (with the exception of the Hastings College of the Law) were treated as off-site departments of the Berkeley campus, and were headed by [provosts](/wiki/Provost_(education) "Provost (education)") who were subordinate to the president. In March 1951, the regents reorganized the university's governing structure. Starting with the 1952–53 academic year, day-to-day "chief executive officer" functions for the Berkeley and Los Angeles campuses were transferred to [chancellors](/wiki/Chancellor_(education) "Chancellor (education)") who were vested with a high degree of autonomy, and reported as equals to UC's president.[[13]](#cite_note-Stadtman2-13)[[14]](#cite_note-MargaretLeslieDavis-14)[[15]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr1-15) As noted above, the regents promoted five additional UC locations to campuses and allowed them to have chancellors of their own in a series of decisions from 1958 to 1964,[[49]](#cite_note-Stadtman3-49) and the three campuses added since then have also been run by chancellors. In turn, all chancellors (again, with the exception of Hastings) report as equals to the University of California President. Today, the UC Office of the President (UCOP) and the Office of the Secretary and Chief of Staff to the Regents of the University of California share an office building in downtown Oakland that serves as the UC system's headquarters.[[65]](#cite_note-UCOPWelcomeGuide-65)

Kerr's vision for UC governance was "one university with pluralistic decision-making".[[66]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr7-66) In other words, the internal delegation of operational authority to chancellors at the campus level and allowing nine other campuses to become separate centers of academic life independent of Berkeley did not change the fact that all campuses remain part of one legal entity. As a 1968 UC centennial [coffee table book](/wiki/Coffee_table_book "Coffee table book") explained: "Yet for all its campuses, colleges, schools, institutes, and research stations, it remains one University, under one Board of Regents and one president—the University of California."[[67]](#cite_note-Pickerell_Page11-67) UC continues to take a "united approach" as one university in matters in which it inures to UC's advantage to do so, such as when negotiating with the legislature and governor in Sacramento.[[66]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr7-66) The University of California continues to manage certain matters at the systemwide level in order to maintain common standards across all campuses, such as student admissions, appointment and promotion of faculty, and approval of academic programs.[[68]](#cite_note-Pelfrey_Page47-68)

All UC presidents had been white men until 2013, when former [Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security") [Secretary](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Homeland_Security "United States Secretary of Homeland Security"), and [Governor of Arizona](/wiki/Governor_of_Arizona "Governor of Arizona"), [Janet Napolitano](/wiki/Janet_Napolitano "Janet Napolitano") became the first woman to hold the office of UC President.[[130]](#cite_note-131) On July 7, 2020, Dr. [Michael V. Drake](/wiki/Michael_V._Drake "Michael V. Drake"), a former UC chancellor and medical research professor, was selected as the 21st president of the University of California system, making him the first black president to hold the office in UC's 152-year history. He took office on August 1, 2020.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

#### Official residences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Official residences")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/University_House_UC_Berkeley.jpg/250px-University_House_UC_Berkeley.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_House_UC_Berkeley.jpg)

[University House, Berkeley](/wiki/University_House,_Berkeley "University House, Berkeley") served as the official residence of the UC President from 1911 until 1958. Today it serves Berkeley's Chancellor.

Besides substantial six-figure incomes, the UC president and all UC chancellors enjoy controversial perks such as free housing in the form of university-maintained mansions.[[132]](#cite_note-sfgate_free_mansions-133) In 1962, Anson Blake's will donated his 10-acre (40,000 m2) estate ([Blake Garden](/wiki/Blake_Garden,_Kensington "Blake Garden, Kensington")) and mansion (Blake House) in [Kensington](/wiki/Kensington,_California "Kensington, California") to the University of California's Department of Landscape Architecture. In 1968, the regents decided to make Blake House the official residence of the UC president. As of 2005, it cost around $300,000 per year to maintain Blake Garden and Blake House; the latter, built in 1926, is a 13,239-square-foot (1,229.9 m2) mansion with a view of San Francisco Bay.[[132]](#cite_note-sfgate_free_mansions-133)

Blake House has sat vacant since President Dynes departed in 2008, due to the high cost of needed seismic strengthening and renovating its dilapidated interior (estimated at $3.5 million in 2013).[[133]](#cite_note-Dinkelspiel-134) From 2008 to 2022, all three UC presidents during that timeframe (i.e., Yudof, Napolitano, and Drake) lived in rented homes.[[133]](#cite_note-Dinkelspiel-134) In 2022, UC finally purchased the [Selden Williams House](/wiki/Selden_Williams_House "Selden Williams House"), a 6,400-square-foot (590 m2) house in Berkeley, for $6.5 million to serve as the UC president's official residence.[[133]](#cite_note-Dinkelspiel-134) UC had previously owned the same home from 1971 to 1991, when it served as the official residence of the UC vice president.[[133]](#cite_note-Dinkelspiel-134) (UC no longer has a single "vice president"; the president's direct reports now have titles like "executive vice president", "senior vice president", or "vice president".[[134]](#cite_note-UCOPOrgChart-135))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/2821_Claremont_Boulevard.jpg/250px-2821_Claremont_Boulevard.jpg)](/wiki/File:2821_Claremont_Boulevard.jpg)

[Selden Williams House](/wiki/Selden_Williams_House "Selden Williams House"), built in 1928 and designed by architect [Julia Morgan](/wiki/Julia_Morgan "Julia Morgan"), serves as the official residence of the UC President, since 2022.

All UC chancellors traditionally live for free in a mansion on or near campus that is usually known as *University House*, where they host social functions attended by guests and donors.[[135]](#cite_note-136) Berkeley's [University House](/wiki/University_House,_Berkeley "University House, Berkeley") formerly served as the official residence of the UC president, but is now the official residence of Berkeley's chancellor. UCSD's [University House](/wiki/Audrey_Geisel_University_House "Audrey Geisel University House") was closed from 2004 to 2014 for $10.5 million in renovations paid for by private donors, which were so expensive because the 12,000-square-foot structure sits on top of a sacred Native American cemetery and next to an unstable coastal bluff.[[136]](#cite_note-Kucher-137)[[137]](#cite_note-Schwab-138) Not all chancellors prefer to live on campus; at Santa Barbara, Chancellor [Robert Huttenback](/wiki/Robert_Huttenback "Robert Huttenback") found that campus's University House to be unsatisfactory, then was convicted in 1988 of embezzlement for his unauthorized use of university funds to improve his off-campus residence.[[138]](#cite_note-Ebenstein-139)

### Finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Finances")]

Main article: [University of California finances](/wiki/University_of_California_finances "University of California finances")

The State of California currently (2021–2022) spends $3.467 billion on the UC system, out of total UC operating revenues of $41.6 billion. The "UC Budget for Current Operations" lists the medical centers as the largest revenue source, contributing 39% of the budget, the federal government 11%, Core Funds (State General Funds, UC General Funds, student tuition) 21%, private support (gifts, grants, endowments) 7%, and Sales and Services at 21%. In 1980, the state funded 86.8% of the UC budget.[[139]](#cite_note-:1-140) While state funding has somewhat recovered, as of 2019 state support still lags behind even recent historic levels (e.g. 2001) when adjusted for inflation.[[139]](#cite_note-:1-140)[[140]](#cite_note-:2-141)

According to the California Public Policy Institute, California spends 12% of its General Fund on higher education, but that percentage is divided between the University of California, California State University and California Community Colleges. Over the past forty years, state funding of higher education has dropped from 18% to 12%, resulting in a drop in UC's per student funding from $23,000 in 2016 to a current $8,000 per year per student.[[141]](#cite_note-142)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/University_of_California_Office_of_the_President.jpg/250px-University_of_California_Office_of_the_President.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_California_Office_of_the_President.jpg)

View of the UC Office of the President.

In May 2004, UC President [Robert C. Dynes](/wiki/Robert_C._Dynes "Robert C. Dynes") and CSU Chancellor [Charles B. Reed](/wiki/Charles_B._Reed "Charles B. Reed") struck a private deal, called the "Higher Education Compact", with Governor [Schwarzenegger](/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger"). They agreed to slash spending by about a billion dollars (about a third of the university's core budget for academic operations) in exchange for a funding formula lasting until 2011. The agreement calls for modest annual increases in state funds (but not enough to replace the loss in state funds Dynes and Schwarzenegger agreed to), private fundraising to help pay for basic programs, and large student fee hikes, especially for graduate and professional students. A detailed analysis of the Compact by the Academic Senate "Futures Report" indicated, despite the large fee increases, the university core budget did not recover to 2000 levels.[[142]](#cite_note-143) Undergraduate student fees have risen 90% from 2003 to 2007.[[143]](#cite_note-144) In 2011, for the first time in UC's history, student fees exceeded contributions from the State of California.[[144]](#cite_note-145)

The [First District Court of Appeal](/wiki/California_courts_of_appeal "California courts of appeal") in San Francisco ruled in 2007 that the University of California owed nearly $40 million in refunds to about 40,000 students who were promised that their tuition fees would remain steady, but were hit with increases when the state ran short of money in 2003.[[145]](#cite_note-146)

In September 2019, the University of California announced it will [divest](/wiki/Fossil_fuel_divestment "Fossil fuel divestment") its $83 billion in endowment and pension funds from the fossil fuel industry, ostensibly to avoid the "financial risk" inherent in that industry because of [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), but also in response to pleas to stop investing in fossil fuel.[[146]](#cite_note-147)

### Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Criticism")]

In 2008, the [Western Association of Schools and Colleges](/wiki/Western_Association_of_Schools_and_Colleges "Western Association of Schools and Colleges"), the regional accreditor of the UC schools, criticized the UC system for "significant problems in governance, leadership and decision making" and "confusion about the roles and responsibilities of the university president, the regents and the 10 campus chancellors with no clear lines of authority and boundaries".[[147]](#cite_note-148)

In 2016, university system officials admitted that they monitored all e-mails sent to and from their servers.[[148]](#cite_note-uofbigbrother-149)

The ten UC campuses

At present, the UC system officially describes itself as a system of "10 campuses" consisting of the campuses listed below.[[149]](#cite_note-150) These campuses are under the direct control of the regents and president.[[150]](#cite_note-151) Only ten campuses are listed on the official UC letterhead.[[151]](#cite_note-152)

Although it shares the name and public status of the UC system, the [College of the Law, San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California_College_of_the_Law,_San_Francisco "University of California College of the Law, San Francisco") (formerly Hastings College of the Law) is not controlled by the regents or president; it has a separate board of directors and must seek funding directly from the Legislature. However, under the California Education Code, Hastings degrees are awarded in the name of the regents and bear the signature of the UC president.[[152]](#cite_note-153) Furthermore, Education Code section 92201 states that Hastings "is affiliated with the University of California, and is the law department thereof".[[153]](#cite_note-154)

### University rankings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: University rankings")]

Annually, UC campuses are ranked highly by various publications. Six UC campuses rank in the top 50 U.S. National Universities of 2026 by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*: [Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"), [UCLA](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles"), [UC Santa Barbara](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara"), [UC San Diego](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego"), [UC Irvine](/wiki/University_of_California,_Irvine "University of California, Irvine"), and [UC Davis](/wiki/University_of_California,_Davis "University of California, Davis"). Four UC campuses also ranked in the top 50 in the *U.S. News & World Report* Best Global Universities Rankings in 2026, namely Berkeley, UCLA, [UCSF](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Francisco "University of California, San Francisco"), and UC San Diego.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] UCSF is ranked as one of the top universities in [biomedicine](/wiki/Biomedicine "Biomedicine") in the world[[154]](#cite_note-155)[[155]](#cite_note-156)[[156]](#cite_note-157)[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159)[[159]](#cite_note-160) and the [UCSF School of Medicine](/wiki/UCSF_School_of_Medicine "UCSF School of Medicine") is ranked 3rd in the United States among research-oriented medical schools and for [primary care](/wiki/Primary_care "Primary care") by *U.S. News & World Report*.[[160]](#cite_note-161)

Three UC campuses: Berkeley, UCLA, and UC San Diego all ranked in the top 15 universities in the US according to the 2020 *[Academic Ranking of World Universities (ARWU)](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities")* US National University Rankings and also in the top 20 in World University Rankings. The *Academic Ranking of World Universities* also ranked UCSF, UC Davis, UC Irvine, and UC Santa Barbara in the top 50 US National Universities and in the top 100 World Universities in 2020.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

Berkeley, UCLA, and UC San Diego all ranked in the top 50 universities in the world according to both the *[Times Higher Education World University Rankings](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings")* for 2021 and the *Center for World University Rankings (CWUR)* for 2020, while UC Irvine, UC Santa Barbara, and UC Davis ranked in the top 100 universities in the world.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* also ranked the six UC campuses mentioned above as being in the top 50 universities in America in 2021.[[161]](#cite_note-:14-162) Forbes also named the top three public universities in America as all being UC campuses, namely, Berkeley, UCLA, and UCSD, and ranked three more campuses, UC Davis, UC Santa Barbara, and UC Irvine as being among the top 20 public universities in America in 2021.[[162]](#cite_note-:15-163) The six aforementioned campuses are all considered [Public Ivies](/wiki/Public_Ivy "Public Ivy").[[6]](#cite_note-:11-6) The *[QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings")* for 2021 ranked three UC campuses: Berkeley, UCLA and UC San Diego as being in the top 100 universities in the world.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

Individual academic departments also rank highly among the UC campuses. The 2021 *U.S. News & World Report* Best Graduate Schools report ranked Berkeley as being among the top 5 universities in the nation in the departments of Psychology, Economics, Political Science, Computer Science, Engineering, Biological Sciences, Chemistry, Mathematics, Earth Sciences, Physics, Sociology, History, and English, and ranked UCLA in the top 20 in the same departments.[[163]](#cite_note-:5-164)[[164]](#cite_note-:6-165) *U.S. News & World Report* also ranked the same departments at UC San Diego among the top 20 in the nation, with the exception of the departments of Sociology, History, and English.[[165]](#cite_note-:7-166) UC Davis, UC Irvine and UC Santa Barbara ranked in the top 50 in the departments of Psychology, Economics, Political Science, Computer Science, Engineering, Biological Sciences, Chemistry, Mathematics, Earth Sciences, Physics, Sociology, History, and English, with the exception of UC Santa Barbara's Psychology and Political Science departments, according to *U.S. News & World Report*. [UC Santa Cruz](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Cruz "University of California, Santa Cruz") and [UC Riverside](/wiki/University_of_California,_Riverside "University of California, Riverside") ranked in the top 100 in the nation in the same departments, along with [UC Merced's](/wiki/University_of_California,_Merced "University of California, Merced") Psychology and Political Science departments.[[166]](#cite_note-:8-167)[[167]](#cite_note-:9-168)[[168]](#cite_note-:10-169)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Academics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Doe_Library_north_side.JPG/250px-Doe_Library_north_side.JPG)](/wiki/File:Doe_Library_north_side.JPG)

[Doe Memorial Library](/wiki/Doe_Memorial_Library "Doe Memorial Library"), main facility of the [UC Berkeley Libraries](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley_Libraries "University of California, Berkeley Libraries").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/UC_Irvine%2C_Langson_Library_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-UC_Irvine%2C_Langson_Library_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:UC_Irvine,_Langson_Library_(cropped).JPG)

[Langson Library](/wiki/Campus_of_the_University_of_California,_Irvine#Libraries_and_study_centers "Campus of the University of California, Irvine") at UC Irvine.

As of the end of fiscal year 2023, UC controls 13,810 active patents. UC researchers and faculty were responsible for 1,440 new inventions that same year.[[2]](#cite_note-At_a_glance-2) On average, UC researchers create four new inventions per day.[[2]](#cite_note-At_a_glance-2)

Eight of UC's ten campuses ([Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"), [UC Davis](/wiki/University_of_California,_Davis "University of California, Davis"), [UCI](/wiki/University_of_California,_Irvine "University of California, Irvine"), [UCLA](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles"), [UC Riverside](/wiki/University_of_California,_Riverside "University of California, Riverside"), [UCSD](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego"), [UC Santa Barbara](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara"), and [UC Santa Cruz](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Cruz "University of California, Santa Cruz")) are members of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") (AAU),[[2]](#cite_note-At_a_glance-2) an alliance of elite American research universities[[178]](#cite_note-Rosenzweig-180) founded in 1900 at UC's suggestion.[[179]](#cite_note-AAU_Founding_Letter-181) Collectively, the system counts among its faculty (as of 2002):

* 389 members of the [Academy of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences "American Academy of Arts and Sciences")
* 5 [Fields Medal](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal") recipients
* 19 [Fulbright](/wiki/Fulbright_Program "Fulbright Program") Scholars
* 25 [MacArthur Fellows](/wiki/MacArthur_Fellows "MacArthur Fellows")
* 254 members of the [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/United_States_National_Academy_of_Sciences "United States National Academy of Sciences")
* 91 members of the [National Academy of Engineering](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Engineering "National Academy of Engineering")
* 13 [National Medal of Science](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Science "National Medal of Science") laureates
* 61 [Nobel laureates](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize")[[8]](#cite_note-UC_Regents-8)
* 106 members of the [Institute of Medicine](/wiki/Institute_of_Medicine "Institute of Medicine")

### UC Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: UC Libraries")]

Main article: [University of California Libraries](/wiki/University_of_California_Libraries "University of California Libraries")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/UC_Santa_Barbara_Library_%2835399729253%29.jpg/250px-UC_Santa_Barbara_Library_%2835399729253%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:UC_Santa_Barbara_Library_(35399729253).jpg)

Davidson Library, the main facility of the [UC Santa Barbara Library](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara_Library "University of California, Santa Barbara Library").

At 40.8 million print volumes,[[181]](#cite_note-183) the University of California library system is home to one of the largest collections of printed materials in the world. On July 27, 2021, all ten campuses went live with a unified online library catalog, UC Library Search. Besides on-campus libraries, the UC system also maintains two regional library facilities (one each for Northern and Southern California), which each accept older items from all UC campus libraries in their respective region. As of 2019, Northern Regional Library Facility is home to 7.4 million items, while Southern Regional Library Facility is home to 6.5 million items.

### Academic calendar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Academic calendar")]

In 1966, UC switched its [academic calendar](/wiki/Academic_term "Academic term") from the semester system to the quarter system. This was "part of a national trend to join state campuses across the U.S. that were aligning with private universities, including [Stanford](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University"), that had operated on quarters since World War I to accommodate students in [military training programs](/wiki/Reserve_Officers%27_Training_Corps "Reserve Officers' Training Corps")".[[182]](#cite_note-Kaleem-184)

Berkeley returned to the semester system in 1983, and Merced has operated on the semester system since its 2005 opening. However, all five law schools operate on the semester system, as does the [David Geffen School of Medicine at UCLA](/wiki/David_Geffen_School_of_Medicine_at_UCLA "David Geffen School of Medicine at UCLA").

In fall 2024, with the quarter system in decline nationwide (down to about 50 institutions and falling), UC initiated a systemwide study of switching all campuses back to the semester system.[[182]](#cite_note-Kaleem-184)

### Academic organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Academic organization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/McHenry_Library_UCSC.jpg/250px-McHenry_Library_UCSC.jpg)](/wiki/File:McHenry_Library_UCSC.jpg)

McHenry Library at UC Santa Cruz.

Davis, Los Angeles, Riverside, and Santa Barbara all followed Berkeley's example by aggregating the majority of [arts, humanities, and science departments](/wiki/College_of_Arts_and_Sciences "College of Arts and Sciences") into a relatively large College of Letters and Science. Therefore, at [Berkeley](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_College_of_Letters_and_Science "UC Berkeley College of Letters and Science"), [Davis](/wiki/UC_Davis_College_of_Letters_and_Science "UC Davis College of Letters and Science"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/UCLA_College_of_Letters_and_Science "UCLA College of Letters and Science"), and [Santa Barbara](/wiki/UCSB_College_of_Letters_and_Science "UCSB College of Letters and Science"), their respective College of Letters and Science is by far the single largest academic unit on each campus. The College of Letters and Science at Los Angeles is the largest academic unit in the entire UC system.[[183]](#cite_note-UCLAGeneralCatalog-185) Riverside later [separated the natural sciences](/wiki/UCR_College_of_Natural_and_Agricultural_Sciences "UCR College of Natural and Agricultural Sciences") and kept only [social sciences grouped with arts and humanities](/wiki/UCR_College_of_Humanities,_Arts,_and_Social_Sciences "UCR College of Humanities, Arts, and Social Sciences"), an example followed by Merced at its founding (although Merced organizes its departments into schools and not colleges).

Due to President Kerr's interest in not reproducing the impersonal undergraduate experience often seen in such gigantic academic units, San Diego and Santa Cruz both implemented [residential college](/wiki/Residential_college "Residential college") systems inspired by British models (in which each college has distinctive general education requirements reflecting its chosen theme)[[184]](#cite_note-ClarkKerr5-186) and grouped most academic departments into a small number of broadly defined divisions which are all independent of the colleges. In February 2022, San Diego turned its divisions into schools.

Irvine is organized into 13 schools and San Francisco is organized into four schools, all of which are relatively narrow in scope. Originally, Irvine was also going to have a College of Letters and Science, like Berkeley. But the original UCI plan, written by [Ivan Hinderaker](/wiki/Ivan_Hinderaker "Ivan Hinderaker"), granted the college's five divisions so much autonomy from one another that by 1967, the UCI Academic Senate voted to transform the divisions into "schools" whose deans would report directly to the vice chancellor for academic affairs.[[185]](#cite_note-Stadtman_Page_419-187)

## Admissions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Admissions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Kerckhoff_Hall_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Kerckhoff_Hall_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kerckhoff_Hall_(cropped).JPG)

Kerckhoff Hall is home of the [Associated Students of the University of California, Los Angeles](/wiki/ASUCLA "ASUCLA").

Each UC campus handles admissions separately, but a student wishing to apply for an undergraduate or transfer admission uses one application for all UC campuses. Graduate and professional school admissions are handled directly and separately by each department or program to which one applies.

In May 2020, UC approved plans to suspend standardized testing score requirements in admissions until 2024.[[186]](#cite_note-188) In May 2021, after a student lawsuit, the University of California announced that it would no longer consider SAT and ACT scores in admissions and scholarship decisions.[[187]](#cite_note-189)

The [Early Academic Outreach Program](/wiki/EAOP "EAOP") (EAOP) was established in 1976 by University of California (UC) in response to the [State Legislature's](/wiki/California_State_Legislature "California State Legislature") recommendation to expand post-secondary opportunities to all of California's students including those who are first-generation, socioeconomically disadvantaged, and English-language learners.[[188]](#cite_note-190) As UC's largest academic preparation program, EAOP assists middle and high school students with academic preparation, admissions requirements, and financial aid requirements for higher education.[[189]](#cite_note-191) The program designs and provides services to foster students' academic development, and delivers those services in partnership with other academic preparation programs, schools, other higher education institutions and community/industry partners.[[190]](#cite_note-192)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Haas_School_of_Business_west_entrance.JPG/250px-Haas_School_of_Business_west_entrance.JPG)](/wiki/File:Haas_School_of_Business_west_entrance.JPG)

[Haas School of Business](/wiki/Haas_School_of_Business "Haas School of Business") at Berkeley is ranked among the best business schools in the world.

The University of California admits a significant number of transfer students primarily from the [California Community Colleges](/wiki/California_Community_Colleges "California Community Colleges").[[191]](#cite_note-:0-193) Approximately one out of three UC students begin at a community college before graduating.[[191]](#cite_note-:0-193) In evaluating a transfer student's application the universities conduct a "comprehensive review" process that includes consideration of grade point averages of the generally required, transferable and or related courses for the intended major. The review may also include consideration of an applicant's enrollment in selective honor courses or programs, extracurricular activities, essay, family history, life challenges, and the location of the student's residence. Different universities emphasize different factors in their evaluations.[[192]](#cite_note-194)

### Freshmen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Freshmen")]

Before 1986, students who wanted to apply to UC for undergraduate study could only apply to one campus. Students who were rejected at that campus but otherwise met the UC minimum eligibility requirements were *redirected* to another campus with available space.[[193]](#cite_note-RobinsonNina-195)[[194]](#cite_note-Trombley2-196) Students who did not want to be redirected were refunded their application fees.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] UC Riverside chancellor [Ivan Hinderaker](/wiki/Ivan_Hinderaker "Ivan Hinderaker") explained in 1972: "Redirection has been a negative rather than a plus. Some come with a [chip on their shoulders](/wiki/Chip_on_shoulder "Chip on shoulder") so big they never give the campus a chance. They poison the attitudes of the students around them."[[194]](#cite_note-Trombley2-196)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Jacobs_School_of_Engineering%2C_UCSD.jpg/250px-Jacobs_School_of_Engineering%2C_UCSD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacobs_School_of_Engineering,_UCSD.jpg)

[Jacobs School of Engineering](/wiki/Jacobs_School_of_Engineering "Jacobs School of Engineering"), at San Diego, is one of the top-ranked engineering schools in the country.

Therefore, in 1986, the undergraduate application system was changed to the current "multiple filing" system, in which students can apply to as many or as few UC campuses as they want on one application, paying a fee for each campus. This significantly increased the number of applications to the Berkeley and Los Angeles campuses, since students could choose a campus to attend after they received acceptance letters, without fear of being redirected to a campus they did not want to attend.[[193]](#cite_note-RobinsonNina-195)

The University of California accepts fully eligible students from among the top one-eighth (1/8) of California public high school graduates through regular statewide admission, or the top 9% of any given high school class through Eligibility in the Local Context (see below). Part of the eligibility process is completion of the [A-G requirements](/wiki/California_A-G_requirements "California A-G requirements") in high school. All eligible California high school students who apply are accepted to the university, though not necessarily to the campus of choice.[[195]](#cite_note-ELC-197)[[196]](#cite_note-198) Eligible students who are not accepted to the campus(es) of their choice are placed in the "referral pool", where campuses with open space may offer admission to those students; in 2003, 10% of students who received an offer through this referral process accepted it.[[197]](#cite_note-199) In 2007, about 4,100 UC-eligible students who were not offered admission to their campus of choice were referred to UC Riverside or the system's newest campus, UC Merced.[[198]](#cite_note-200)

As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit) the *[San Francisco Chronicle](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle "San Francisco Chronicle")* defines "competitive UCs" as UCLA, Berkeley, Irvine, Davis, Santa Barbara, and San Diego, with UCLA and Berkeley the most selective. Santa Cruz, Riverside, and Merced are the least selective.[[199]](#cite_note-zakharenko20260305-201) In 2015, all UC-eligible students rejected by their campus of choice were redirected to UC Merced, which is now the only campus that has space for all qualified applicants.[[200]](#cite_note-202)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/UCLA_School_of_Law_library_tower_2_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-UCLA_School_of_Law_library_tower_2_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:UCLA_School_of_Law_library_tower_2_(cropped).jpg)

[UCLA School of Law](/wiki/UCLA_School_of_Law "UCLA School of Law") is one of the top ranked law schools in the United States.

The old undergraduate admissions were conducted on a two-phase basis. In the first phase, students were admitted based solely on academic achievement. This accounted for between 50 and 75% of the admissions. In the second phase, the university conducted a "comprehensive review" of the student's achievements, including extracurricular activities, essay, family history, and life challenges, to admit the remainder. Students who did not qualify for regular admission were "admitted by exception"; in 2002, approximately 2% of newly admitted undergraduates were admitted by exception.[[201]](#cite_note-203)

The process for determining admissions varies. At some campuses, such as Santa Barbara and Santa Cruz, a point system is used to weight [grade point average](/wiki/Grading_in_education "Grading in education"), [SAT](/wiki/SAT "SAT") Reasoning or [ACT](/wiki/ACT_(examination) "ACT (examination)") scores, and SAT Subject scores, while at San Diego, Berkeley, and UCLA, academic achievement is examined in the context of the school and the surrounding community, known as a holistic review.

[Race](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_Census "Race and ethnicity in the United States Census"), [gender](/wiki/Gender "Gender"), [national origin](/wiki/Nationality "Nationality"), and [ethnicity](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group") were not used as UC admission criteria due to the passing of [Proposition 209](/wiki/California_Proposition_209_(1996) "California Proposition 209 (1996)"). This information was collected for statistical purposes.

Eligibility in the Local Context, commonly referred to as ELC, is met by applicants ranked in the top 9% of their high school class in terms of performance on an 11-unit pattern of UC-approved high school courses. Beginning with fall 2007 applicants, ELC also requires a UC-calculated GPA of at least 3.0. Fully eligible ELC students are guaranteed a spot at one of UC's undergraduate campuses, though not necessarily at their first-choice campus or even to a campus to which they applied.[[195]](#cite_note-ELC-197)

In 2021, the University of California freshmen class was its most diverse and largest ever, with 84,223 students.[[202]](#cite_note-Diverse-204) Latinos were the largest group at 37%; Asian Americans at 34%; white non-Hispanics at 20%; African-Americans at 5%; and 4% composed of American Indians, Pacific Islanders or those who declined to state their race or ethnicity.[[202]](#cite_note-Diverse-204)
