### Admissions practices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Admissions practices")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Lawn_-_University_of_California%2C_Davis_-_DSC03312_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Lawn_-_University_of_California%2C_Davis_-_DSC03312_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lawn_-_University_of_California,_Davis_-_DSC03312_(cropped).JPG)

Mrak Hall serves as the administrative seat of UC Davis.

In many recent years, the University of California has faced growing criticism for high admissions of out-of-state or international students as opposed to in-state, California students. In particular, UC Berkeley and UCLA have been heavily criticized for this phenomenon due to their extraordinarily low acceptance rates compared to other campuses in the system.[[206]](#cite_note-208) At a Board of Regents meeting in 2015, California Governor [Jerry Brown](/wiki/Jerry_Brown "Jerry Brown") reportedly said about the problem: "And so you got your foreign students and you got your 4.0 folks, but just the kind of ordinary, normal students, you know, that got good grades but weren't at the top of the heap there—they're getting frozen out."[[207]](#cite_note-209) State lawmakers have proposed legislation that would reduce out-of-state admission.[[208]](#cite_note-210)

A 2020 California [auditor's report](/wiki/Auditor%27s_report "Auditor's report") indicated that at least 64 wealthy students were wrongfully admitted to UC schools as favors to powerful figures.[[209]](#cite_note-211)[[210]](#cite_note-212)[[211]](#cite_note-213) Many of the admissions were justified by falsely classifying the applicants as student athletes. The incidents disproportionately (55 of 64) occurred at [UC Berkeley](/wiki/UC_Berkeley "UC Berkeley").

## Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Research")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Qualcomm_Institute.jpg/250px-Qualcomm_Institute.jpg)](/wiki/File:Qualcomm_Institute.jpg)

The [California Institute for Telecommunications and Information Technology](/wiki/California_Institute_for_Telecommunications_and_Information_Technology "California Institute for Telecommunications and Information Technology"), jointly run by UC San Diego, UC Irvine, and UC Riverside.

In 2006 the [Scholarly Publishing and Academic Resources Coalition (SPARC)](/wiki/Scholarly_Publishing_and_Academic_Resources_Coalition "Scholarly Publishing and Academic Resources Coalition") awarded the University of California the SPARC Innovator Award for its "extraordinarily effective institution-wide vision and efforts to move [scholarly communication](/wiki/Scholarly_communication "Scholarly communication") forward", including the 1997 founding (under then UC President [Richard C. Atkinson](/wiki/Richard_C._Atkinson "Richard C. Atkinson")) of the [California Digital Library (CDL)](/wiki/California_Digital_Library "California Digital Library") and its 2002 launching of CDL's [eScholarship](/wiki/California_Digital_Library#eScholarship "California Digital Library"), an [institutional repository](/wiki/Institutional_repository "Institutional repository"). The award also specifically cited the widely influential 2005 [academic journal publishing reform](/wiki/Academic_journal_publishing_reform "Academic journal publishing reform") efforts of UC faculty and librarians in "altering the marketplace" by publicly negotiating contracts with publishers, as well as their 2006 proposal to amend UC's [copyright](/wiki/Copyright "Copyright") policy to allow [open access](/wiki/Open_access "Open access") to UC faculty research.[[212]](#cite_note-SPARC-214)

On July 24, 2013, the UC Academic Senate adopted an [Open Access Policy](/wiki/Open-access_mandate "Open-access mandate"), mandating that all UC faculty produced research with a publication agreement signed after that date be first deposited in UC's eScholarship [open access repository](/wiki/Open_access_repository "Open access repository").[[213]](#cite_note-215)

University of California systemwide research on the [SAT](/wiki/SAT "SAT") exam found that, after controlling for familial income and parental education, so-called achievement tests known as the SAT II had 10 times more predictive ability of college aptitude than the SAT I.[[214]](#cite_note-216)

## Peripheral enterprises

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Peripheral enterprises")]

The University of California has a long tradition of involvement in many enterprises that are often geographically or organizationally separate from its general campuses, including national laboratories, observatories, hospitals, continuing education programs, hotels, conference centers, an airport, a seaport, and an art institute.

### National laboratories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: National laboratories")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Molecular_Foundry_at_Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory_%2852230758982%29.jpg/250px-Molecular_Foundry_at_Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory_%2852230758982%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Molecular_Foundry_at_Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory_(52230758982).jpg)

[Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory") in the [Berkeley Hills](/wiki/Berkeley_Hills "Berkeley Hills").

The University of California directly manages and operates one [United States Department of Energy National Laboratory](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy_National_Laboratories "United States Department of Energy National Laboratories"):[[215]](#cite_note-217)

* [Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory") (LBNL, or Berkeley Lab) ([Berkeley, California](/wiki/Berkeley,_California "Berkeley, California"))

UC is a limited partner in two separate private [limited liability companies](/wiki/Limited_liability_company "Limited liability company") that manage and operate two other Department of Energy national laboratories:

* [Los Alamos National Laboratory](/wiki/Los_Alamos_National_Laboratory "Los Alamos National Laboratory") (LANL) ([Los Alamos, New Mexico](/wiki/Los_Alamos,_New_Mexico "Los Alamos, New Mexico")) operated by Triad National Security, LLC.
* [Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Livermore_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory") (LLNL) ([Livermore, California](/wiki/Livermore,_California "Livermore, California")) operated by Lawrence Livermore National Security, LLC.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/University_of_California_Radiation_Laboratory_staff_on_the_magnet_yoke_for_the_60-inch_cyclotron%2C_1938.jpg/250px-University_of_California_Radiation_Laboratory_staff_on_the_magnet_yoke_for_the_60-inch_cyclotron%2C_1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_California_Radiation_Laboratory_staff_on_the_magnet_yoke_for_the_60-inch_cyclotron,_1938.jpg)

Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory staff on the magnet yoke for the 60-inch cyclotron, 1938; Nobel prize winners [Ernest Lawrence](/wiki/Ernest_Lawrence "Ernest Lawrence"), [Edwin McMillan](/wiki/Edwin_McMillan "Edwin McMillan"), and [Luis Alvarez](/wiki/Luis_Walter_Alvarez "Luis Walter Alvarez") are shown, in addition to [J. Robert Oppenheimer](/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer "J. Robert Oppenheimer") and [Robert R. Wilson](/wiki/Robert_R._Wilson "Robert R. Wilson").

The **Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory** conducts unclassified research across a wide range of scientific disciplines with key efforts focused on fundamental studies of the universe, quantitative biology, nanoscience, new energy systems and environmental solutions, and the use of integrated computing as a tool for discovery.

The **Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory** uses advanced science and technology to ensure that U.S. nuclear weapons remain reliable. LLNL also has major research programs in supercomputing and predictive modeling, energy and environment, bioscience and biotechnology, basic science and applied technology, counter-proliferation of weapons of mass destruction, and homeland security. It is also home to the most powerful supercomputers in the world.

The **Los Alamos National Laboratory** focuses most of its work on ensuring the reliability of U.S. nuclear weapons. Other work at LANL involves research programs into preventing the spread of weapons of mass destruction and US national security, such as protection of the US homeland from terrorist attacks.

The UC system's ties to the three laboratories have occasionally sparked controversy and protest, because all three laboratories have been intimately linked with the development of [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon"). During the [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project"), Lawrence Berkeley Lab developed the electromagnetic method for the separation of uranium isotopes used to develop the first atomic bombs. The Los Alamos and Lawrence Livermore labs have been involved in designing U.S. nuclear weapons from their inception until the shift into [stockpile stewardship](/wiki/Stockpile_stewardship "Stockpile stewardship") after the end of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). Historically the two national laboratories in Berkeley and Livermore named after [Ernest O. Lawrence](/wiki/Ernest_O._Lawrence "Ernest O. Lawrence"), have had very close relationships on research projects, as well as sharing some business operations and staff. In fact, [LLNL](/wiki/LLNL "LLNL") was not officially severed administratively from [LBNL](/wiki/LBNL "LBNL") until the early 1970s. They also have much deeper ties to the university than the Los Alamos Lab, a fact seen in their respective original names; the University of California Berkeley Radiation Laboratory and the University of California Radiation Laboratory at Livermore.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Lick_Observatory_Shane_Telescope.jpg/250px-Lick_Observatory_Shane_Telescope.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lick_Observatory_Shane_Telescope.jpg)

[Lick Observatory](/wiki/Lick_Observatory "Lick Observatory"), atop [Mount Hamilton](/wiki/Mount_Hamilton_(California) "Mount Hamilton (California)") in the [Diablo Range](/wiki/Diablo_Range "Diablo Range").

The UC system's ties to the labs have so far outlasted all periods of internal controversy. However, in 2003, the U.S. Department of Energy for the first time opened the [Los Alamos National Laboratory](/wiki/Los_Alamos_National_Laboratory "Los Alamos National Laboratory") (LANL) contract for bidding by other vendors. UC entered into a partnership with [Bechtel Corporation](/wiki/Bechtel_Corporation "Bechtel Corporation"), [BWXT](/wiki/BWXT "BWXT"), and the [Washington Group International](/wiki/Washington_Group_International "Washington Group International"), and together they created a private company called [Los Alamos National Security, LLC](/wiki/Los_Alamos_National_Security,_LLC "Los Alamos National Security, LLC") (LANS). The only other bidder on the LANL contract was a [Lockheed Martin](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin "Lockheed Martin") Corporation-created company that included, among others, the [University of Texas System](/wiki/University_of_Texas_System "University of Texas System"). In December 2005, a seven-year contract to manage the laboratory was awarded to the Los Alamos National Security, LLC.[[216]](#cite_note-218) On October 1, 2007, the University of California ended its direct involvement in operating the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory. Management of the laboratory was taken over by Lawrence Livermore National Security, LLC, a limited liability company whose members are Bechtel National, the University of California, Babcock & Wilcox, the Washington Division of URS Corporation, Battelle Memorial Institute, and The Texas A&M University System. Other than UC appointing three members to the two separate boards of directors (each with eleven members) that oversee LANS and LLNS, UC now has virtually no responsibility for or direct involvement in either LANL or LLNL. UC policies and regulations that apply to UC campuses and the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory in California no longer apply to LANL and LLNL, and the LANL and LLNL directors no longer report to the UC Regents or UC Office of the President.

### Observatories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Observatories")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Keck_and_NASA_Telescopes._Mauna_Kea_Summit_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Keck_and_NASA_Telescopes._Mauna_Kea_Summit_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Keck_and_NASA_Telescopes._Mauna_Kea_Summit_-_panoramio.jpg)

[Keck Observatory](/wiki/W._M._Keck_Observatory "W. M. Keck Observatory"), atop [Mauna Kea](/wiki/Mauna_Kea "Mauna Kea") volcano on the [Big Island](/wiki/Hawaii_(island) "Hawaii (island)") of Hawaii.

The University of California manages two [observatories](/wiki/Observatory "Observatory") as a multi-campus research unit headquartered at [UC Santa Cruz](/wiki/UC_Santa_Cruz "UC Santa Cruz").

* [Lick Observatory](/wiki/Lick_Observatory "Lick Observatory") atop [Mount Hamilton](/wiki/Mount_Hamilton_(California) "Mount Hamilton (California)"), in the [Diablo Range](/wiki/Diablo_Range "Diablo Range") just east of [San Jose](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California").
* [Keck Observatory](/wiki/Keck_Observatory "Keck Observatory") at the 4,145 meters (13,599 feet) summit of [Mauna Kea](/wiki/Mauna_Kea "Mauna Kea") in Hawaii.

### High-performance networking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: High-performance networking")]

The University of California is a founding and charter member of the [Corporation for Education Network Initiatives in California](/wiki/Corporation_for_Education_Network_Initiatives_in_California "Corporation for Education Network Initiatives in California"), a nonprofit organization that provides high-performance Internet-based networking to California's K-20 research and education community.

### UC Natural Reserve System

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: UC Natural Reserve System")]

Main article: [University of California Natural Reserve System](/wiki/University_of_California_Natural_Reserve_System "University of California Natural Reserve System")

The [NRS](/wiki/University_of_California_Natural_Reserve_System "University of California Natural Reserve System") was established in January 1965 to provide UC faculty with large areas of land where they could conduct long-term ecosystem research without having to worry about outside disturbances like tourists. Today, the NRS manages 39 reserves that total more than 756,000 acres (3,060 km2).

### UC Agriculture and Natural Resources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: UC Agriculture and Natural Resources")]

University of California Agriculture and Natural Resources[[217]](#cite_note-UCANR-219) (UCANR, Division of Agriculture and Natural Resources) plays an important role in the state's agriculture industry, as mandated by UC's legacy as a land-grant institution. In addition to conducting agriculture and [Youth development](/wiki/Youth_development "Youth development") research, every county in the state has a [cooperative extension](/wiki/Cooperative_extension "Cooperative extension") office with county farm advisors. The county offices also support [4-H](/wiki/4-H "4-H") programs and have nutrition, family, and consumer sciences advisors who assist local government. Currently, the division's University of California 4-H Youth Development Program[[218]](#cite_note-220) is a national leader in studying [thriving](/wiki/Thriving "Thriving") in the field of youth development.[[219]](#cite_note-221)

### Other national research centers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Other national research centers")]

From September 2003 to July 2016, UC managed a contract valued at more than $330 million to establish and operate a University Affiliated Research Center (UARC) at the [NASA Ames Research Center](/wiki/NASA_Ames_Research_Center "NASA Ames Research Center") at [Moffett Federal Airfield](/wiki/Moffett_Federal_Airfield "Moffett Federal Airfield") —the largest grant ever awarded the university. [UC Santa Cruz](/wiki/UC_Santa_Cruz "UC Santa Cruz") managed the UARC for the University of California, with the goal of increasing the science output, safety, and effectiveness of [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA")'s missions through new technologies and scientific techniques.

Since 2002, the [NSF](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation")-funded [San Diego Supercomputer Center](/wiki/San_Diego_Supercomputer_Center "San Diego Supercomputer Center") at [UC San Diego](/wiki/UC_San_Diego "UC San Diego") has been managed by the University of California, which took over from the previous manager, [General Atomics](/wiki/General_Atomics "General Atomics").

### Medical centers and schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Medical centers and schools")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/UCSF_Benioff_Children%27s_Hospital%2C_Mission_Bay_%28July_2020%29_-2.jpg/250px-UCSF_Benioff_Children%27s_Hospital%2C_Mission_Bay_%28July_2020%29_-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:UCSF_Benioff_Children%27s_Hospital,_Mission_Bay_(July_2020)_-2.jpg)

[UCSF Benioff Children's Hospital](/wiki/UCSF_Benioff_Children%27s_Hospital "UCSF Benioff Children's Hospital").

The University of California operates five medical centers throughout the state:

* [UC Davis Medical Center](/wiki/UC_Davis_Medical_Center "UC Davis Medical Center"), in [Sacramento](/wiki/Sacramento,_California "Sacramento, California");
* [UC Irvine Medical Center](/wiki/UC_Irvine_Medical_Center "UC Irvine Medical Center"), in [Orange](/wiki/Orange,_California "Orange, California");
* [UCLA Medical Center](/wiki/UCLA_Health_System "UCLA Health System"), comprising two distinct hospitals:
  + [Ronald Reagan UCLA Medical Center](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_UCLA_Medical_Center "Ronald Reagan UCLA Medical Center"), in Los Angeles;
  + [UCLA Santa Monica Medical Center](/wiki/UCLA_Medical_Center,_Santa_Monica "UCLA Medical Center, Santa Monica"), in [Santa Monica](/wiki/Santa_Monica,_California "Santa Monica, California");
* [UC San Diego Medical Center](/wiki/UC_San_Diego_Health "UC San Diego Health"), comprising two distinct hospitals:
  + [UC San Diego Medical Center, Hillcrest](/wiki/UC_San_Diego_Medical_Center,_Hillcrest "UC San Diego Medical Center, Hillcrest"), in [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego");
  + [Jacobs Medical Center](/wiki/Jacobs_Medical_Center "Jacobs Medical Center"), in [La Jolla](/wiki/La_Jolla "La Jolla"); and
* [UCSF Medical Center](/wiki/UCSF_Medical_Center "UCSF Medical Center"), operating as a single medical center across three physically distinct campuses around San Francisco.

  [UCI Medical Center](/wiki/UC_Irvine_Medical_Center "UC Irvine Medical Center")

There are two medical centers that bear the UCLA name, but are not operated by UCLA: [Harbor–UCLA Medical Center](/wiki/Harbor%E2%80%93UCLA_Medical_Center "Harbor–UCLA Medical Center") and [Olive View–UCLA Medical Center](/wiki/Olive_View%E2%80%93UCLA_Medical_Center "Olive View–UCLA Medical Center"). They are actually [Los Angeles County](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County,_California "Los Angeles County, California")-operated facilities that UCLA uses as [teaching hospitals](/wiki/Teaching_hospital "Teaching hospital").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Mattel_UCLA_Hospital.jpg/250px-Mattel_UCLA_Hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mattel_UCLA_Hospital.jpg)

[UCLA Mattel Children's Hospital](/wiki/UCLA_Mattel_Children%27s_Hospital "UCLA Mattel Children's Hospital").

Each medical center serves as the primary teaching site for that campus's medical school. UCSF is perennially among the top five programs in both research and primary care, and both UCLA and UC San Diego consistently rank among the top fifteen research schools, according to annual rankings published by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*.[[220]](#cite_note-222) The teaching hospitals affiliated with each school are also highly regarded – the [UCSF Medical Center](/wiki/UCSF_Medical_Center "UCSF Medical Center") was ranked the number one hospital in California and number 5 in the country by *U.S. News & World Report*'s 2017 Honor Roll for Best Hospitals in the United States.[[221]](#cite_note-223) UC also has a sixth medical school—[UC Riverside School of Medicine](/wiki/UC_Riverside_School_of_Medicine "UC Riverside School of Medicine"), the only one in the UC system without its own hospital.

In the latter half of the 20th century, the UC hospitals became the cores of full-fledged regional health systems; they were gradually supplemented by many outpatient clinics, offices, and institutes. Three UC hospitals are actually county hospitals that were sold to UC, which means that UC currently plays a major role in providing healthcare to the indigent. The medical hospitals operated by UC Irvine (acquired in 1976), UC Davis (acquired in 1978), and UC San Diego (acquired in 1984) each began as the respective county hospitals of [Orange County](/wiki/Orange_County,_California "Orange County, California"), [Sacramento County](/wiki/Sacramento_County,_California "Sacramento County, California"), and [San Diego County](/wiki/San_Diego_County,_California "San Diego County, California"). As of 2025, UC medical centers handle each year about 10.8 million outpatient visits, 474,000 emergency room visits, and roughly 1.32 million inpatient days.[[2]](#cite_note-At_a_glance-2)

### Facilities outside of California

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Facilities outside of California")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Casa_de_California_en_M%C3%A9xico.jpg/250px-Casa_de_California_en_M%C3%A9xico.jpg)](/wiki/File:Casa_de_California_en_M%C3%A9xico.jpg)

Casa de California in [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City").

UC operates several other miscellaneous sites to support faculty, students, and researchers away from its general campuses:

* UC Berkeley operates the Richard B. Gump South Pacific Research Station in [Mo'orea](/wiki/Mo%27orea "Mo'orea"), [French Polynesia](/wiki/French_Polynesia "French Polynesia") on land donated in 1981 by the heir to the founder of the [Gump's](/wiki/Gump%27s "Gump's") home furnishings store.[[222]](#cite_note-224)
* UC Davis operates UC Center Sacramento, which supports students from all UC campuses who are [interning](/wiki/Internship "Internship") with the [California government](/wiki/Government_of_California "Government of California").
* UC Davis operates UC Davis Chile Life Sciences Innovation Center in [Providencia, Chile](/wiki/Providencia,_Chile "Providencia, Chile"), with support from Chilean government agency [CORFO](/wiki/CORFO "CORFO"). The center is a nonprofit foundation which coordinates [technology transfers](/wiki/Technology_transfer "Technology transfer") between Davis and partner institutions in Chile. It is the latest in a series of "[technical assistance](/wiki/Development_aid "Development aid")" programs between the California and Chile governments, dating back to the Chile-California Program of 1963.[[223]](#cite_note-Chile-225)
* UC Irvine founded UC Washington Center (UCDC) in the federal capital at Washington, D.C. UCDC includes a dormitory to host UC students interning with the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"). It is now jointly operated and supported by all nine UC campuses which admit undergraduates.
* UC Riverside currently operates one overseas site on behalf of the systemwide Alianza MX program:
  + Casa de California in [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City").
* UC Office of the President's Education Abroad Program (EAP) briefly operated California House in London during the early-to-mid 2000s.

### Hospitality facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Hospitality facilities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Ellen_Browning_Scripps_Memorial_Pier%2C_La_Jolla_%2C_United_States_%28Unsplash%29.jpg/250px-Ellen_Browning_Scripps_Memorial_Pier%2C_La_Jolla_%2C_United_States_%28Unsplash%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ellen_Browning_Scripps_Memorial_Pier,_La_Jolla_,_United_States_(Unsplash).jpg)

[Scripps Institution of Oceanography](/wiki/Scripps_Institution_of_Oceanography "Scripps Institution of Oceanography") pier, in [La Jolla](/wiki/La_Jolla "La Jolla").

Unlike other land-grant institutions (e.g., [Cornell](/wiki/Cornell_University_School_of_Hotel_Administration "Cornell University School of Hotel Administration")) UC does not provide a [hospitality management](/wiki/Hospitality_management_studies "Hospitality management studies") program, but it does provide general hospitality at some locations:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* UC Berkeley's Cal Alumni Association operates travel excursions for alumni (and their families) under its "Cal Discoveries Travel" brand (formerly BearTreks); many of the tour guides are Berkeley professors. CAA also operates the oldest and largest alumni association-run family camp in the world, the Lair of the Golden Bear. Located at an altitude of 5600 feet in [Pinecrest, California](/wiki/Pinecrest,_Tuolumne_County,_California "Pinecrest, Tuolumne County, California"), the Lair is a home-away-from-home for almost 10,000 campers annually. Its attendees are largely Cal alumni and their families, but the Lair is open to everyone.
* Berkeley Lab operates its own hotel, the Berkeley Lab Guest House, available to persons with business at the Lab itself or UC Berkeley.
* The UCLA Hospitality Group operates two on-campus hotels, the 61-room Inn at UCLA and the 254-room Meyer and Renee Luskin Conference Center, and the UCLA Lake Arrowhead Lodge at [Lake Arrowhead](/wiki/Lake_Arrowhead,_California "Lake Arrowhead, California") (with a mix of chalet-like condominiums, lodge rooms, and stand-alone cottages). During the summer, Lake Arrowhead hosts the Bruin Woods vacation programs for UCLA alumni and their families.
* Separately, UCLA Health operates the 100-room Tiverton House just south of the UCLA campus to serve its patients and their families.
* UC Santa Cruz leased the University Inn and Conference Center in downtown Santa Cruz from 2001 to 2011 for use as off-campus student housing.

### Seaport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Seaport")]

UC San Diego's [Scripps Institution of Oceanography](/wiki/Scripps_Institution_of_Oceanography "Scripps Institution of Oceanography") owns a seaport, the Nimitz Marine Facility, which is just south of Shelter Island on [Point Loma](/wiki/Point_Loma "Point Loma"), San Diego. The port is used as an operating base for all of its oceanographic vessels and platforms.

### UC Extension

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: UC Extension")]

For over a century, the university has operated a continuing education program for working adults and professionals. At present, UC Extension has enrolled over 700,000 students, (500,000 of which are unique) in over 3,000 courses, with approximately 100,000 students attending during 2022–2023. One of the reasons for its large size is that UC Extension is an approved and the dominant provider of education for [WIOA](/wiki/Workforce_Innovation_and_Opportunity_Act "Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act") and [TAAP](/wiki/Trade_Adjustment_Assistance "Trade Adjustment Assistance") workers in California.[[224]](#cite_note-226) Also, the systemwide portion of UC Extension (directly controlled by the UC Office of the President) operates [Continuing Education of the Bar](/wiki/CEB_(Continuing_Education_of_the_Bar) "CEB (Continuing Education of the Bar)") under a joint venture agreement with the [State Bar of California](/wiki/State_Bar_of_California "State Bar of California").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
